title: GDL Path
keyPoint: Class 7 is the first GDL step: learner, newly licensed, then full licence.
summary: Nova Scotia uses Graduated Driver Licensing so new drivers build skill in stages. Restrictions are removed only after you pass the required tests and gain experience.
handbookSection: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence, The Nova Scotia Licensing System

title: Zero Alcohol
keyPoint: Learners must keep a zero blood alcohol level every time they drive.
summary: A Class 7 learner has a stricter standard than the Criminal Code limit. Any alcohol in the body can put your learner licence at risk.
handbookSection: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence, Learner's Licence Restrictions

title: Transit Bus Yield
keyPoint: Yield to a stopped transit bus signalling back into traffic where the limit is 60 km/h or less.
summary: When approaching from behind, slow and let the bus re-enter if it is safe. This update applies to transit buses, not school buses.
handbookSection: Introduction — Driver's Handbook Updates, Yield to Transit Buses

title: Two Test Parts
keyPoint: You must pass rules of the road and road sign recognition as separate parts.
summary: The Class 7 knowledge test checks traffic laws, safe driving, and road signs. A miss on one part means you only retake the part you did not pass.
handbookSection: Official Class 7 Knowledge Test — What You're Tested On

title: Class 7 Eligibility
keyPoint: You can apply at 16; age 16 or 17 needs parent or guardian consent.
summary: Class 7 is for Nova Scotia residents who meet the learner requirements. Bring required identification and complete the Registry steps before the licence is issued.
handbookSection: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence, Getting a Learner's Licence

title: Pass Mark
keyPoint: Each test part has 20 questions; you need at least 16 correct on each.
summary: The learner knowledge test has a rules test and a signs test. Study both because passing one part does not cover a fail on the other.
handbookSection: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence, How to Apply for a Learner's Licence

title: Learner Supervisor
keyPoint: Drive only with an experienced licensed driver in the front seat.
summary: A learner cannot drive alone. The supervising driver must be ready to help, and learner passenger limits still apply.
handbookSection: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence, Learner's Licence Restrictions

title: Demerit Points
keyPoint: Demerit points hit learners and newly licensed drivers sooner than experienced drivers.
summary: Convictions under the Motor Vehicle Act can add points to your record. Serious or repeated offences can delay progress through GDL.
handbookSection: Chapter 1 — Your Driver's Licence, Demerit Points

title: Traffic Lights
keyPoint: Green means go only when safe; yellow means stop if safe; red means stop.
summary: Do not enter an intersection just because the light is green. A flashing red works like a stop sign, and a flashing yellow means slow and proceed carefully.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Traffic Signal Lights

title: Crosswalk Priority
keyPoint: Always yield to pedestrians in marked or unmarked crosswalks.
summary: Intersections include crosswalks even when the paint is not visible. If a signal changes while someone is crossing, let them finish safely.
imageAlt: Pedestrian crosswalk sign with a walking person symbol
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Pedestrians and Crosswalks

title: Intersection Yield
keyPoint: First in the intersection goes first; when arriving together, yield to the right.
summary: Right-of-way rules prevent guessing at intersections. Yield signs and stop signs can change the order, so obey the posted control first.
imageAlt: Red and white triangular yield sign
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Right-of-Way at Intersections

title: Emergency Vehicles
keyPoint: Move right and stop for lights and sirens until the emergency vehicle passes.
summary: Leave the route clear and do not follow a responding fire vehicle within 150 metres. Also keep clear of stopped emergency vehicles assisting at collisions.
handbookSection: Chapter 2 — Rules of the Road, Emergency Vehicles

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Stop Signs
keyPoint: Stop completely, yield, then move only when the way is clear.
summary: A stop sign requires a full stop at the stop line, crosswalk, or edge of the road. Rolling through is not a stop.
imageAlt: Red octagonal stop sign with white STOP lettering
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Warning Signs
keyPoint: Yellow warning signs tell you to adjust before a hazard or road change.
summary: Warning signs can show curves, merging traffic, narrow roads, crossings, or slippery surfaces. Slow early and choose a safe lane position.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Warning Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Double Solid Lines
keyPoint: Do not cross continuous double solid yellow lines to pass.
summary: Yellow lines separate traffic moving in opposite directions. Broken lines may permit passing when safe, but double solid yellow lines mean no passing either way.
imageAlt: Pavement marking diagram with double solid yellow lines
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Pavement Markings

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Work Zones
keyPoint: Orange signs mean temporary conditions: slow down, watch workers, and expect lane shifts.
summary: Work zones can change traffic flow quickly. Leave extra space, obey traffic control people, and follow temporary signs even if they differ from the usual road setup.
imageAlt: Orange human-activity sign showing workers active
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Construction / Work Zone Signs

title: Vehicle Check
keyPoint: Check brakes, tires, lights, mirrors, horn, and wipers before problems find you.
summary: A road-ready vehicle gives you time to react. Regular maintenance also reduces preventable breakdowns and failed inspections.
handbookSection: Chapter 4 — Safety, Vehicle Maintenance

title: Seat Belts
keyPoint: Seat belts keep occupants in place; airbags do not replace them.
summary: Belts and child restraints reduce injury in a sudden stop or crash. Everyone should be properly restrained before the vehicle moves.
handbookSection: Chapter 4 — Safety, Passenger Safety

title: Blind Spots
keyPoint: Mirrors help, but shoulder checks confirm the space beside you is clear.
summary: Set your mirrors before moving and scan them often. Always shoulder check before changing lanes, turning, or pulling away from the curb.
handbookSection: Chapter 4 — Safety, Defensive Driving and Blind Spots

title: ABS Braking
keyPoint: With ABS, press the brake firmly and steadily; do not pump it.
summary: Anti-lock brakes help you keep steering control during hard braking. If the vehicle does not have ABS, use controlled braking to avoid locking the wheels.
handbookSection: Chapter 4 — Safety, Emergency Braking

title: Night Driving
keyPoint: Slow enough that you can stop within the distance your headlights show.
summary: Night driving reduces visibility and reaction time. Dim high beams for oncoming vehicles and when following another vehicle closely.
handbookSection: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions, Nighttime Driving

title: Bad Weather
keyPoint: Poor visibility or traction means slow down and increase following distance.
summary: Rain, snow, fog, and spray can hide hazards and lengthen stopping distance. Use low-beam headlights when visibility is reduced.
handbookSection: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions, Rain, Snow, and Fog

title: Hydroplaning
keyPoint: If tires ride on water, ease off the accelerator and steer straight.
summary: Hydroplaning removes tire contact with the road. Bridges, overpasses, and shaded areas can also freeze first, so treat them cautiously in cold weather.
handbookSection: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions, Rain

title: Brake Failure
keyPoint: Pump the brake pedal; if needed, apply the parking brake gently.
summary: Try to rebuild pressure by pumping the pedal several times. Hold the parking brake release while applying it gently so the wheels do not lock.
handbookSection: Chapter 5 — Adverse Driving Conditions, Brake Failure

title: First Drink Counts
keyPoint: Alcohol impairment starts with the first drink, not at a magic number.
summary: Drivers can be unsafe before reaching a criminal BAC threshold. Learners and other GDL drivers have a zero-alcohol requirement.
handbookSection: Chapter 6 — Driving and Impairment, Drinking and Driving

title: Medication Warning
keyPoint: Prescription, non-prescription, herbal, and illegal drugs can all impair driving.
summary: Read labels and ask a pharmacist if a medicine may cause drowsiness, slow reactions, or poor judgment. Never mix impairing substances with driving.
handbookSection: Chapter 6 — Driving and Impairment, Drug Impairment

title: Warn Range
keyPoint: A roadside result from 0.05 to 0.08 can still lead to a suspension.
summary: You do not need to be at 0.08 BAC to face consequences. For learners, the practical target is simple: zero alcohol.
handbookSection: Chapter 6 — Driving and Impairment, Drinking and Driving

title: Sober Ride Plan
keyPoint: Plan the ride home before drinking or using any impairing substance.
summary: Judgment drops as impairment rises, so do not wait until later to decide. Use a sober driver, taxi, transit, or stay overnight.
handbookSection: Chapter 6 — Driving and Impairment, Being a Responsible Host or Hostess

title: Road-Ready Documents
keyPoint: A vehicle needs registration, plates, inspection, and insurance before highway use.
summary: Keep required proof available when driving. The vehicle must be legal to operate, not just mechanically able to move.
handbookSection: Chapter 7 — Vehicle Registration and Insurance, Registering a Vehicle

title: Registration Certificate
keyPoint: The certificate identifies the owner, vehicle, VIN, and registration details.
summary: Registration proves the vehicle is recorded with the province. Update ownership or vehicle information when required instead of relying on outdated papers.
handbookSection: Chapter 7 — Vehicle Registration and Insurance, Certificate of Registration

title: Safety Inspection
keyPoint: Registered vehicles and trailers need a valid safety inspection.
summary: Official inspection stations check key safety equipment. If a vehicle fails, defects must be repaired and the vehicle reinspected within the allowed period.
handbookSection: Chapter 7 — Vehicle Registration and Insurance, Safety Inspection

title: Liability Insurance
keyPoint: Carry proof of financial responsibility; the handbook lists $500,000 minimum liability.
summary: Drivers must have accepted insurance or proof of financial responsibility. Insurance is a legal requirement, not an optional add-on.
handbookSection: Chapter 7 — Vehicle Registration and Insurance, Insurance

title: Motorcycle Licence
keyPoint: Motorcycle riders need a motorcycle licence or endorsement.
summary: A regular passenger-vehicle licence alone is not enough to ride a motorcycle. New motorcycle riders complete motorcycle-specific tests and GDL steps.
handbookSection: Chapter 8 — Motorcycles, Getting a Motorcycle Licence

title: Motorcycle Learner Limits
keyPoint: No passengers, zero alcohol, and daylight-only riding for motorcycle learners.
summary: A motorcycle learner may ride only from one half-hour before sunrise to one half-hour after sunset. These limits reduce risk while skills are new.
handbookSection: Chapter 8 — Motorcycles, Learner Motorcycle Restrictions

title: Motorcycle Lane Space
keyPoint: A motorcycle is entitled to the full use of a traffic lane.
summary: Do not crowd motorcycles or share their lane. Riders should stay visible and avoid lingering in other drivers' blind spots.
handbookSection: Chapter 8 — Motorcycles, Lane Position and Visibility

title: Helmet Rule
keyPoint: Motorcycle riders and passengers must wear approved, fastened helmets.
summary: Eye protection and protective clothing also reduce injury and improve control. Gear matters because riders have far less crash protection than car occupants.
handbookSection: Chapter 8 — Motorcycles, Protective Clothing

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Do Not Enter
keyPoint: Do not enter that road, ramp, or lane from your direction.
summary: This regulatory sign protects against wrong-way movement. Stop and choose another legal route instead of continuing past the sign.
imageAlt: Red do-not-enter sign with a white horizontal bar
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Regulatory Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: School Zones
keyPoint: When children are present: 30 km/h in 50 zones, 50 km/h where the approach limit is higher.
summary: School and playground signs mean children may move unpredictably. Slow early, scan both sides of the road, and be ready to stop.
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, School and Playground Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Flashing Arrow Boards
keyPoint: Arrow boards warn of lane closures or obstructions; pass on the indicated side.
summary: A two-arrow board means either side is safe to pass. A straight bar is caution mode and does not tell you that passing is safe.
imageAlt: Flashing arrow board with two arrow heads
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Flashing Light Units

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Railway Crossings
keyPoint: Slow, look, listen, and be ready to stop at railway crossings.
summary: A crossbuck marks the crossing itself, and an advance sign warns one is ahead. Stop when lights flash, a gate is down, or a train is approaching.
imageAlt: White railway crossbuck sign with red border
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Railway Signs

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Information Signs
keyPoint: Route and direction signs help you choose lanes and exits early.
summary: Green, blue, and white signs can show highway numbers, destinations, services, and distances. Use them early so you avoid last-second lane changes.
imageAlt: Provincial highway route marker for Highway 102
handbookSection: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones, Information and Direction Signs
